question: Your tee shot lands in a yellow penalty area (water hazard). What are your options?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1d** - "The player may play the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" and "keeping the estimated point where the original ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area between the hole and the spot where the ball is dropped" **Ruling**: You have two relief options from a yellow penalty area, each with a one-stroke penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: (1) Stroke-and-distance relief: Play from where your last stroke was made, adding one penalty stroke. (2) Back-on-the-line relief: Drop outside the penalty area keeping the crossing point between the hole and drop spot, adding one penalty stroke. Unlike red penalty areas, lateral relief is not available. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-17
response:
RULING: You have several options under the penalty area relief rule:

You may take relief by dropping a ball in the relief area under penalty of one stroke. The relief area is either:
1) Within two club-lengths of and not nearer the hole than the point where the ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area, or
2) On a line from the hole through the point where the ball last crossed the edge, with no limit on how far back you may drop.

Alternatively, you may play the ball as it lies within the penalty area without penalty. This carries the risk that the ball may be unplayable.

If your ball is not found within the three-minute search time, you may also proceed under the lost ball rule by going back to the tee and playing under penalty of stroke and distance.

Let me know if you need any clarification on the procedures for these relief options.

question: You hit your tee shot and it goes out of bounds. What must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 18** - "the player must take stroke-and-distance relief by adding one penalty stroke and playing the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" **Ruling**: When your ball goes out of bounds, you must take stroke-and-distance relief. **Penalties/Procedures**: Add one penalty stroke to your score and play another ball from the tee (where the previous stroke was made). A ball is out of bounds only when all of it is outside the boundary edge of the course. The original ball must be abandoned once you put another ball into play. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: Under the out of bounds rule, you must play a ball under penalty of stroke and distance.

This means you must go back and play another ball from the spot where you played your previous stroke, adding one penalty stroke to your score. You have the following options:

1. Play a ball from the teeing area, adding one penalty stroke to your score.
2. Drop a ball in the relief area under the stroke and distance relief procedure, which is within two club-lengths of the point where your previous stroke was made, not nearer the hole. This also adds one penalty stroke.

The out of bounds rule requires you to go back and re-play your shot, adding a one-stroke penalty. This ensures you take the appropriate penalty for the lost ball that went out of bounds.

question: Your ball is in a greenside bunker. Can you remove a small stone lying next to your ball before playing your shot?
ground_truth: **Rule 12.2a** - "Before playing a ball in a bunker, a player may remove loose impediments under Rule 15.1 and movable obstructions under Rule 15.2" **Ruling**: Yes, you can remove loose impediments like stones from bunkers without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for removing stones or other loose impediments from bunkers. Incidental contact with sand during removal is permitted. However, you cannot deliberately touch sand to test conditions or during practice swings (Rule 12.2b - general penalty applies). **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-12
response:
RULING: Yes, you may remove the small stone lying next to your ball in the bunker before playing your shot.

The rules governing touching sand in a bunker were changed in the 2019 revision. Players are now allowed to touch sand in a bunker with their hand or club, as long as it is not for the purpose of testing the condition of the bunker or touching sand in the area right behind or in front of the ball, or during a practice swing or the backstroke.

Removing a loose impediment, such as a small stone, from beside your ball in a bunker is permitted under the rules. You are allowed to do this without penalty.

question: Your ball comes to rest on a paved cart path in the fairway. Are you entitled to free relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1** - "You are only allowed to take free relief from a cart path if it is artificially surfaced" **Ruling**: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from an artificially surfaced (paved) cart path, which is classified as an immovable obstruction. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Locate the nearest point where you no longer have interference, drop within one club-length of that point not nearer the hole. The ball must stay in the same area of the course. Natural grass or sand paths do not qualify for relief. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/articles/taking-free-relief-under-the-rules
response:
RULING: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from the paved cart path.

The rules regarding relief from abnormal course conditions, such as cart paths, allow you to take free relief. Since your ball is on a paved cart path in the fairway, you may take relief without penalty.

The relevant rule is the "Abnormal Course Conditions" rule. This allows you to take free relief if your ball lies in or touches an abnormal course condition, which includes paved cart paths. You may take relief by dropping your ball, without penalty, within one club-length of the nearest point of complete relief, not nearer the hole.

Once you have determined the nearest point of complete relief, you should mark that spot, lift your ball, and drop it within one club-length of the marked spot, not nearer the hole. The ball must then come to rest in that relief area.

question: Your ball is on the putting green. Can you repair spike marks in your line of putt?
ground_truth: **Rule 13.1c** - "Damage on the putting green includes shoe damage (such as spike marks) and scrapes or indentations caused by equipment or a flagstick" **Ruling**: Yes, you can repair spike marks on the putting green, including in your line of putt. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for repairing spike marks. Use your hand, foot, body part, ball-mark repair tool, tee, club or similar equipment. Must not unreasonably delay play. Cannot repair natural surface imperfections, aeration holes, or damage from normal maintenance practices. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-13
response:
RULING: Yes, you may repair spike marks or other damage on the putting green that is on your line of putt.

The rules allow players to repair any damage on the putting green that might affect the line of play, including spike marks, ball marks, and other abnormal damage. This is covered under the rule governing maintenance of the putting green.

The procedure is to carefully repair the damage using an appropriate tool, such as a tee or pitch mark repair tool, without unduly delaying play. The player should be careful not to improve the lie of the ball or the area of intended swing.

This exception allowing repair of damage on the putting green is intended to help maintain the integrity of the putting surface and fairness of the play. It does not apply to the general area, bunkers, or other parts of the course.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both make a 5 on a hole. What is the result of the hole?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2a(2)** - "The player and opponent complete the hole in the same number of strokes (including strokes made and penalty strokes)" **Ruling**: The hole is halved (tied). **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. When both players complete a hole with the same score, the hole is halved and neither player wins the hole. Players can also agree to halve a hole after at least one player has made a stroke to begin the hole. The match status remains unchanged for that hole. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3
response:
RULING: The hole is halved in match play.

In match play, when both players make the same score on a hole, the hole is considered "halved". This means the match is tied for that hole, and the score remains the same as it was before playing that hole. Neither player wins or loses the hole.

The key difference from stroke play is that in match play, the goal is to win more holes than your opponent, rather than having the lowest total score. So if you both make the same score on a hole, the hole is considered a tie or "halved" between the two players.

question: You hit your ball into thick rough and cannot find it. How long do you have to search before it is considered lost?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.2a(1)** - "A ball is lost if not found in three minutes after the player or their caddie begins to search for it" **Ruling**: You have three minutes to search for your ball before it is considered lost. **Penalties/Procedures**: If not found in three minutes, the ball is lost and you must take stroke-and-distance relief by returning to where your previous stroke was made and adding one penalty stroke. If found within three minutes but identity is uncertain, you get reasonable additional time to identify it. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: The ball is lost if it is not found within three minutes after the player or their caddie began searching for it.

Under the lost ball rule, a player has three minutes to search for their ball after arriving at the area where they believe the ball is likely to be. If the ball is not found within those three minutes, it is considered lost. The player must then proceed under the stroke-and-distance relief procedure, going back to the previous spot and playing again under penalty of one stroke.

The three-minute search time applies whether the ball is lost in the rough, a penalty area, or anywhere else on the course. It is a universal time limit for a lost ball. The player or their caddie can search anywhere they believe the ball may be during that three-minute window.

If the ball is found after the three minutes has elapsed, it is too late - the ball is considered lost and the player must proceed under stroke and distance. The three-minute search time is strictly enforced.

question: In match play, your putt from the green hits the flagstick that was left in the hole. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty. In match play, the player may have the flagstick attended, removed, or left in the hole while making a stroke on the putting green.

The rules governing the flagstick on the putting green are different in match play versus stroke play. In match play, Rule 13.2b(2) allows the player to have the flagstick attended, removed, or left in the hole while making a stroke. There is no penalty if the ball hits the unattended flagstick.

This is a specific exception in match play, as the general rule is that a ball striking the unattended flagstick results in a one-stroke penalty (Rule 13.2a(2)). However, that penalty does not apply in match play.

The player is free to choose whether to have the flagstick attended, removed, or left in, with no penalty for it being struck by the ball on the putting green in a match play format.

question: In match play, your opponent concedes your next putt. Can you refuse the concession and putt anyway?
response:
RULING: No, in match play you cannot refuse a concession made by your opponent.

In match play, a player may concede a stroke, a hole, or the entire match at any time. This concession cannot be declined or withdrawn by the player whose stroke or hole has been conceded. The Rules of Golf explicitly state that a concession must be accepted.

So if your opponent concedes your next putt in a match play format, you must accept that concession. You cannot insist on putting out the ball yourself, even if you want the practice or feel you can make the putt. The concession stands, and the hole is complete.

This is a key difference from stroke play, where players are always responsible for completing each hole. In match play, the match format allows for concessions that the player receiving the concession must accept.

question: You think your tee shot might be lost in the woods. You announce you will play a provisional ball and hit another. Your original is then found in the fairway. Which ball do you play?
response:
RULING: Since the original ball was found in the fairway, you must abandon the provisional ball and continue play with the original ball.

The provisional ball rules state that you may play a provisional ball when your original ball might be lost outside a penalty area or might be out of bounds. However, once the original ball is found, the provisional ball must be abandoned. You must continue play with the original ball.

The key factors here are:

1) The original ball was found, rather than lost. When a ball is found, the player must continue with the original ball.

2) The original ball was found in the fairway, not in a penalty area or out of bounds. Since the original is in a playable area, you must use it.

3) A provisional ball is only allowed when the original ball might be lost or out of bounds. Once the original is found in a playable area, the provisional is no longer needed or allowed.

So in this scenario, you must abandon the provisional ball and continue play with the original ball found in the fairway.

question: You take a free drop from an immovable obstruction. You drop the ball from knee height and it bounces and rolls outside the one-club-length relief area. What must you do?
response:
RULING: Since the ball did not come to rest within the one-club-length relief area after being dropped, you must re-drop the ball.

When taking relief from an immovable obstruction, the player must drop the ball within the specified relief area, which is one club-length from the nearest point of complete relief, not nearer the hole. If the ball does not come to rest within that relief area after being dropped, the player must re-drop the ball.

Under the dropping procedure rule, if the ball rolls outside the relief area after being dropped, it must be re-dropped. The player gets a total of two drops to get the ball to come to rest within the relief area. If it does not after the second drop, the player must place the ball where it first touched the ground on the second drop.

So in this case, since the ball rolled out of the relief area after the first drop, you must re-drop the ball within the one-club-length relief area. If it does not stay within the area after the second drop, you would then place the ball where it first touched the ground on the second drop.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a bunker. You decide to take back-on-the-line relief. Must you drop the ball inside the bunker or can you drop outside it?
response:
RULING: You may drop the ball outside the bunker when taking back-on-the-line relief for an unplayable ball in a bunker.

Under the unplayable ball rule, if your ball is in a bunker and you determine it is unplayable, you have three relief options:

1) Take a one-stroke penalty and drop a ball within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball lay, but not nearer the hole and still in the bunker.

2) Take a one-stroke penalty and drop a ball outside the bunker, keeping the point where the ball lay directly between the hole and the spot on which the ball is dropped, with no limit to how far back you can go on that line.

3) Take a two-stroke penalty and play from the original spot under stroke and distance.

When choosing the back-on-the-line relief option (2), you are allowed to drop the ball outside the bunker. You do not have to keep the ball in the bunker. The key is that the spot where you drop must be on the line between the hole and where the original unplayable ball was.

So in this case, you can take back-on-the-line relief and drop the ball outside the bunker, as long as you keep that line between the hole and the original spot.

question: Your ball lies in the general area just outside a GUR boundary, but your stance would be inside the GUR. Are you entitled to free relief?
response:
RULING: No, you are not entitled to free relief from the GUR in this situation. The relief for GUR only applies if your ball is lying in the GUR, not if just your stance would be inside the GUR.

The rules governing GUR (ground under repair) state that a player is entitled to free relief if their ball is lying in the GUR. However, if the ball is outside the GUR boundary, but the player's stance or swing would be impacted by the GUR, there is no free relief available.

In this case, since your ball is outside the GUR boundary, even though your stance would be inside the GUR, you do not qualify for free relief under the GUR rule. You would have to play the ball as it lies, or take relief under a different rule if applicable, such as an abnormal course condition.

question: While searching for your ball in the rough, you accidentally kick it. What is the penalty and what must you do?
response:
RULING: You incur a one-stroke penalty for causing your ball at rest to move, and you must replace the ball on its original spot.

Since you accidentally kicked your ball while searching for it in the rough, you are deemed to have caused the ball to move. Under the rule governing ball moved by player, this results in a one-stroke penalty. You must then replace the ball on its original spot before the stroke was made.

The key points are:
- If a player's actions, including addressing the ball or searching around it, cause the ball to move, that is considered the player having caused the movement.
- This results in a one-stroke penalty.
- The ball must then be replaced on its original spot before the stroke was made.

This applies anywhere on the course, not just the putting green. The rules do not distinguish between accidentally kicking the ball versus any other action that causes it to move - the penalty is the same.

question: In match play, you play out of turn from the tee. Can your opponent make you replay the shot?
response:
RULING: In match play, if a player plays out of turn from the teeing area, the opponent may require the player to replay the shot in correct order.

In match play, the player who has the honor (the right to play first) has significantly more influence over the pace and strategy of the hole than in stroke play. Therefore, the Rules of Golf give the opponent the option to require the player to replay the shot in the proper order.

This is covered under the "Order of Play" rule in match play (Rule 6.4b). If a player plays out of turn from the teeing area, the opponent may immediately require the player to play again in the correct order before the opponent makes their stroke.

This is a key difference from stroke play, where there is no penalty and no option to make the player replay the shot out of turn. In stroke play, the ball is simply played as it lies.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You find your ball sitting on dry land with a playable lie. Are you required to take a penalty drop, or can you play the ball as it lies?
response:
RULING: You may play the ball as it lies without penalty.

When your ball is in a red penalty area, the rule for a red penalty area allows you to play the ball as it lies without penalty. Since you have found your ball sitting on dry land with a playable lie within the red penalty area, you may play the ball from that spot.

The rules governing red penalty areas (previously known as "lateral water hazards") state that if your ball is in a red penalty area, you have three options:

1. Play the ball as it lies without penalty.
2. Take relief by dropping a ball under penalty of one stroke, either (a) within two club-lengths of where the ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area, or (b) on the opposite side of the penalty area equidistant to the hole.
3. Take relief by going back on a straight line from the hole, keeping the point where the ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area between you and the hole, and dropping within one club-length.

Since you have a playable lie on dry ground in the penalty area, you may simply play the ball as it lies with no penalty strokes required.

question: A movable obstruction (a rake) is lying in a bunker. Your ball is not in the bunker but the rake is on your line of play. Can you move the rake?
response:
RULING: Yes, you may move the rake.

The rules governing movable obstructions allow you to move any movable obstruction, including a rake, if it interferes with your area of intended stance or swing, or your line of play. Since the rake is lying in the bunker but your ball is outside the bunker, you may lift and move the rake to any position that does not create a new interference.

There is no penalty for moving a movable obstruction in this situation. Once you have finished your stroke, you must return the rake to its original position if reasonable, unless doing so would again create interference.

question: You start your round with 14 clubs. On the 5th hole you discover that one of the clubs belongs to another player and was placed in your bag by mistake. How many clubs do you have for the purpose of the 14-club limit?
response:
RULING: You have 13 clubs for the purpose of the 14-club limit.

Since you started your round with 14 clubs, but then discovered that one of them belongs to another player, you must remove that club from your bag. The rule governing the 14-club limit is based on the number of clubs you have during the round, not just at the start. So for the remainder of the round, you are limited to 13 clubs.

question: In match play, you realize on the 4th tee that you have been playing with 15 clubs since the start of the round. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: In match play, the penalty for having more than 14 clubs in your bag is loss of hole for each hole where the excess club was used. The player must immediately stop using the excess club(s).

In match play, the Rules of Golf require that a player's golf bag contain no more than 14 clubs. If a player starts a hole with more than 14 clubs, and this is discovered before the player makes a stroke with the 15th club, the penalty is loss of the hole.

The player is responsible for the composition of their golf bag and the number of clubs they carry. If a violation is discovered, the player must immediately stop using the excess club(s) for the remainder of the round.

The penalty applies retroactively to each hole where the excess club was used. So if the 15th club was first used on the 1st hole, the player loses the 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 4th holes, before continuing play with 14 clubs.

This penalty for excess clubs is specific to match play. In stroke play, the penalty is a 2-stroke penalty for each hole where the excess club was used, with a maximum of 4 strokes.

question: Your ball is embedded in the face of a bunker, above the sand, in a grassy area that is part of the bunker wall. Are you entitled to free relief for an embedded ball?
response:
RULING: Yes, you are entitled to free relief for an embedded ball in this situation.

The embedded ball rule applies anywhere in the general area, including embedded balls in the face or wall of a bunker, as long as the ball is embedded in a part of the course that is not cut to fairway height or less. Since this grassy area on the bunker face is not cut to fairway height, the embedded ball rule allows you to take free relief.

To take relief, you would lift the ball, identify it as yours, and then drop it within one club-length of the spot where it was embedded, not nearer the hole, in the general area. You may clean the ball before dropping it.

The embedded ball rule is found in Rule 16.3, which states that free relief is allowed when a ball is embedded in its own pitch-mark in any area of the course except the teeing area. There is no restriction on this relief only applying to closely mown areas - it covers all parts of the general area, including bunker faces and walls that are grassy and not cut to fairway height.

question: You play a provisional ball from the tee. Your provisional lands in a red penalty area. Before searching for your original, you play a second provisional from the tee. Is the second provisional valid?
response:
RULING: The second provisional ball played is not valid. A player may only play a provisional ball when the original ball might be lost outside a penalty area or may be out of bounds. Once the player has determined the original ball is in a penalty area, they are no longer allowed to play a provisional ball.

The rules governing provisional balls state that a player may play a provisional ball if they believe the original ball might be lost outside a penalty area or may be out of bounds. However, if the player then finds that the original ball is in a red (lateral) penalty area, the provisional ball must be abandoned and the player must proceed under the penalty area rules with the original ball.

Since the first provisional ball landed in the penalty area, the player is no longer allowed to play a second provisional. The proper procedure would be to:
1. Locate the original ball in the penalty area
2. Decide whether to play the original ball from the penalty area or take relief
3. If taking relief, do so under the penalty area relief options (stroke-and-distance, back-on-the-line, or lateral relief)

The second provisional ball played in this scenario is not a valid ball in play. The player must proceed under the penalty area rules with their original ball.

question: Your ball is at rest on the putting green. A strong gust of wind moves it into a bunker. Must you play it from the bunker or can you replace it on the green?
response:
RULING: The ball must be replaced on its original spot on the putting green.

The rules regarding a ball at rest being moved by natural forces apply differently on the putting green compared to elsewhere on the course. 

On the putting green, if a ball at rest is moved by wind, water, or other natural forces, the player must replace the ball on its original spot, with no penalty. This is a specific exception to the general rule that a ball moved by natural forces must be played from its new position.

However, this exception only applies if the ball was already at rest on the putting green. If the player had previously lifted and replaced the ball on the green, and then natural forces moved it, the exception would not apply - the ball would have to be played from its new position.

But in this case, since the ball was at rest on the putting green when the wind moved it into the bunker, the player must simply replace the ball on its original spot on the green, with no penalty.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You decide to take lateral relief, but the nearest point within two club-lengths is on the other side of the penalty area. Can you drop there?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot drop on the other side of the penalty area when taking lateral relief from a red penalty area.

The rule for lateral relief from a red penalty area (Rule 17.1d(2)) requires you to drop the ball in this relief area:
- Within two club-lengths of the nearest point of complete relief, 
- Not nearer the hole than the nearest point of complete relief,
- In the general area.

Since the nearest point of complete relief is on the other side of the penalty area, you cannot reach it within two club-lengths. The only valid relief area is on the same side of the penalty area as your original ball. You must drop the ball in that relief area, even if it means dropping it very close to or even in the penalty area itself.

The rule does not allow you to drop on the opposite side, as that would be too far from the nearest point of relief. You are confined to the relief area on the same side as your original ball.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both play wrong balls on a par 3. You each realize the mistake before teeing off on the next hole. What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: In match play, if both players play a wrong ball, the penalty is loss of hole for each player.

The specific rule here is that in match play, if a player plays a wrong ball, the player loses the hole. Since both you and your opponent played wrong balls, the result is that you both lose the hole.

This is different from stroke play, where the penalty for playing a wrong ball is two strokes and the player must correct the error by playing the correct ball. But in match play, the penalty is more severe - loss of the hole.

Since you both realized the mistake before starting the next hole, the proper procedure is to simply move on to the next hole, with the outcome of the previous hole being a loss for each of you. There is no opportunity to correct the wrong ball and try to halve the hole.

The key differences here are:
- In match play, playing a wrong ball results in loss of hole
- This applies to both players, not just the one who played the wrong ball
- The mistake must be caught before starting the next hole

So in summary, in this match play scenario, you and your opponent each lose the hole for playing wrong balls.

question: Your ball is in the general area and an immovable obstruction (a sprinkler head) interferes with your stance. However, the obstruction is inside a red penalty area while your ball is outside it. Can you take free relief from the obstruction?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot take free relief from the immovable obstruction (sprinkler head) in this situation.

The rules on relief from an immovable obstruction state that free relief is allowed only when the obstruction physically interferes with the player's stance or the area of their intended swing. However, since the obstruction (the sprinkler head) is located inside the penalty area while your ball is outside the penalty area, the obstruction interference rules do not apply.

The key factor here is that the obstruction is within the boundaries of the penalty area. When an immovable obstruction is located inside a penalty area, the player must proceed under the penalty area relief rules, not the immovable obstruction rules. Since your ball is outside the penalty area, you do not have the option to take free relief from the obstruction.

Your options in this situation are:
1) Play the ball as it lies, making your best effort to avoid the obstruction.
2) Take penalty area relief, dropping a ball within two club-lengths of the nearest point of complete relief, but not nearer the hole. This involves a one-stroke penalty.
3) If the obstruction is deemed to be so interfering that you cannot make a fair stroke, you can declare the ball unplayable and take unplayable ball relief, also with a one-stroke penalty.

But you cannot take free relief from the immovable obstruction in this case, since it is located inside the penalty area boundary while your ball is outside.

question: Your ball is plugged in the steep face of a bunker. You declare it unplayable and choose lateral relief (two club-lengths). All points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker in the general area. Where must you drop?
response:
RULING: You must drop your ball in the general area outside the bunker, no nearer the hole, within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball was plugged in the bunker face.

The unplayable ball rule allows you to take relief in one of three ways:
1) Stroke-and-distance (return to where you last played from)
2) Back-on-the-line relief (drop behind the unplayable spot, keeping it between you and the hole)
3) Lateral relief (drop within two club-lengths of the unplayable spot, not nearer the hole)

Since all points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker, you must take lateral relief in the general area. The ball must be dropped and come to rest in the general area, not in the bunker itself.

The key factors here are:
- The ball is plugged in the steep face of the bunker
- You have declared it unplayable
- You are choosing the lateral relief option
- All relief options are outside the bunker in the general area

So you will drop your ball in the general area, within two club-lengths of the plugged spot, not nearer the hole. The ball must come to rest in the general area, not in the bunker.

question: In stroke play, you putt from on the green and your ball is still moving when it hits another player's ball that was at rest on the green. What happens?
response:
RULING: The player's ball is played as it lies. There is no penalty.

When a player's ball in motion accidentally hits another ball at rest on the putting green, the player incurs no penalty. The ball is played as it lies after the collision. This applies regardless of whether the other ball was on the player's intended line or not.

The rule governing this situation is the rule on ball striking another ball. This rule states that if a player's ball in motion accidentally strikes another ball, there is no penalty. The ball struck is played from its new position. 

The only exception is if the player's ball was deliberately played to strike the other ball. In that case, the player would incur a penalty of 2 strokes in stroke play (or loss of hole in match play). But an accidental collision on the putting green has no penalty.

So in this scenario, the player's ball is simply played from wherever it comes to rest after striking the other ball. No penalty strokes are applied.

question: In match play, you are 3 down with 3 holes to play. On the 16th tee, your opponent concedes the hole. On the 17th, you win the hole. You are now 1 down with 1 to play. On the 18th green, your opponent concedes your birdie putt, tying the match. Your opponent then says they want to withdraw the concession on hole 16 because they made it by mistake. Can they?
response:
RULING: No, the opponent cannot withdraw the concession of the 16th hole.

In match play, a concession of a hole or a stroke is binding and cannot be withdrawn once made. The opponent's concession of the 16th hole was an act that could not be undone, even if it was made in error. Once a player concedes a stroke or a hole, the result stands.

The key rules governing this situation are:

- In match play, a player may concede a stroke or a hole at any time (Rule 3.2b).
- A concession cannot be declined or withdrawn once made (Rule 3.2b(2)).
- If a player concedes a hole, the result of that hole is final, and the concession cannot be withdrawn (Rule 3.2b(3)).

Therefore, the opponent's attempt to withdraw the concession on the 16th hole is not permitted under the Rules of Golf. The match must be concluded with the concession of the 16th hole standing, resulting in the match being tied after the 18th hole.

question: Your ball is in the general area and you have interference from an animal hole. The nearest point of complete relief would be on a putting green (not the green of the hole being played). Where do you take relief?
response:
RULING: You take relief from the animal hole in the general area by dropping your ball in the relief area, not on the putting green.

The rules governing interference from an abnormal course condition (such as an animal hole) in the general area do not allow you to take relief on the putting green. The nearest point of complete relief must be in the general area, not on the putting green.

Specifically, the relief procedure is:

1. Determine the nearest point of complete relief in the general area, not nearer the hole.
2. Drop the ball within one club-length of that point, in the general area.
3. The ball must come to rest in the relief area.

You cannot take relief by dropping on the putting green, even if that is the nearest point of complete relief. The rules restrict relief from abnormal course conditions to being taken in the same area of the course where the interference exists.

Rule reference: Relief from Abnormal Course Conditions (including Immovable Obstructions) in the General Area (Rule 16.1)

question: In match play, you hit your approach shot and it lands on the green. Your opponent, who hasn't played yet, picks up your ball thinking it's theirs. They realize the mistake and replace it. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty in this situation.

In match play, if a player lifts or marks an opponent's ball on the putting green, mistakenly believing it to be their own ball, there is no penalty. The ball must simply be replaced on its original spot. This is a specific exception in the Rules of Golf.

Since your opponent realized their mistake and replaced your ball, no further action is required. The hole continues with no penalty assessed.

This is different from a situation where a player deliberately lifts or moves an opponent's ball on the putting green, which would result in the loss of the hole. But in this case, it was an honest mistake, so there is no penalty.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. Before playing your next stroke, you ground your club in the penalty area and take a practice swing that touches the ground. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for grounding your club or taking a practice swing in the penalty area.

The current Rules of Golf allow players to ground their club and take practice swings in a penalty area. This was changed in the 2019 rules revision - previously, grounding the club or taking a practice swing in a water hazard (now called a penalty area) would have incurred a one-stroke penalty. But that penalty no longer applies.

Since your ball is in a red penalty area, you may ground your club and take practice swings in the penalty area without penalty. You should proceed by taking relief under the penalty area relief rule, which allows you to drop a ball within two club-lengths of the edge of the penalty area, no nearer the hole, under a one-stroke penalty.

question: You take relief from a cart path. You determine the nearest point of complete relief and drop within one club-length. The ball comes to rest in a position where you now have interference from a sprinkler head. Must you play it as it lies, or can you take additional relief from the sprinkler head?
response:
RULING: You are entitled to take additional relief from the sprinkler head interference, as long as your ball does not lie in a penalty area.

When you took relief from the cart path, you were entitled to find the nearest point of complete relief and drop within one club-length of that spot, no nearer the hole. However, this relief procedure does not guarantee that the ball will come to rest free of any other interference, such as from a sprinkler head.

The Interference with Ball Relief Rule (Rule 16.1) allows you to take free relief from interference by an abnormal course condition, such as a sprinkler head, if:

1) The condition physically interferes with your ability to play your ball as it lies, and 
2) The condition is in the general area (not in a penalty area).

Since the sprinkler head is interfering with your play, and your ball is not in a penalty area, you are entitled to find the nearest point of complete relief from the sprinkler interference and drop a ball within one club-length of that spot, no nearer the hole.

This additional relief from the sprinkler head is free - there is no penalty. You have now completed the full relief procedure from both the cart path and the sprinkler interference.

question: In match play, on the final hole, both players are on the green. Player A is 20 feet away, Player B is 6 feet away. Player A putts first and misses, leaving a 2-foot putt. Player B then concedes Player A's next putt. Player A picks up their ball. Player B then three-putts from 6 feet. Player A claims they win the hole because Player B made 5 and their conceded putt means they made 4. Player B says the hole was halved because the concession happened before B finished. Who is correct?
response:
RULING: Player A is correct. They win the hole.

In match play, a player may concede a stroke, a hole, or the match at any time. A concession cannot be declined or withdrawn.

When Player B conceded Player A's next putt, that ended the hole. Player A's score for the hole was 4 (missed putt plus conceded putt), while Player B's score was 5 (three-putted). Therefore, Player A wins the hole.

The fact that Player B had not finished the hole by three-putting is irrelevant. The concession by Player B terminated the hole, and Player A's score of 4 was better than Player B's score of 5.

So in this case, even though Player B continued playing out the hole after conceding, the concession stands, and Player A wins the hole.

question: Your ball is in a bunker. Before your stroke, you smooth footprints in the bunker that are 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play. You do this to care for the course. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for smoothing the footprints in the bunker that are not on your line of play.

The rules governing touching sand in a bunker were revised in 2019. Players are now allowed to touch sand in a bunker with their hand or club, as long as they are not testing the condition of the bunker or touching sand in the area right behind or in front of the ball, or during a practice swing or the backstroke.

Since the footprints you smoothed were 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play, this is permitted under the current rules. There is no penalty for caring for the course in this way, as long as you do not improve the conditions around your ball.

question: In stroke play, you hit your tee shot into the woods. You don't play a provisional. After searching for 2 minutes, your playing partner finds a ball. You identify it as yours but it's in an unplayable lie. You declare it unplayable and take lateral relief, dropping within two club-lengths. After you play your next shot, you discover the ball you found was actually not yours (the markings were similar). What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: Since the ball you took unplayable relief for was not your original ball, you must now take stroke-and-distance relief for the original ball that is lost.

You did not play a provisional ball after your tee shot went into the woods. When a player's original ball is lost outside a penalty area, they must take stroke-and-distance relief by going back to the previous spot and playing again under penalty of one stroke.

The fact that you later found a ball with similar markings and incorrectly identified it as your own does not change the underlying fact that your original ball is lost. You are still required to go back and play again under stroke-and-distance.

The unplayable ball relief you took was for the wrong ball, so that procedure does not apply here. You must return to the tee and play again under penalty of one stroke.

question: In match play, your opponent's tee shot goes into a red penalty area. They drop under penalty of one stroke and hit their third shot onto the green. You then notice that when they dropped, they dropped on the wrong side of the penalty area (the side closer to the hole). You say nothing until after they two-putt for a 5. You then raise the issue. Is your opponent's score valid?
response:
RULING: The opponent's score of 5 is not valid. The opponent must correct the error by dropping again on the correct side of the penalty area under penalty of one stroke.

In match play, if a player makes a mistake in taking relief under the penalty area rules, the opponent must raise the issue before the player makes a stroke on the next hole. Since you noticed the incorrect drop before the hole was completed, you can still call the violation.

The penalty area relief procedure requires the player to drop on the opposite side of the penalty area from where the original ball last crossed the edge, under penalty of one stroke. Dropping on the side closer to the hole does not satisfy this requirement.

Therefore, the opponent must go back, drop again correctly, and complete the hole. The result of the hole stands once the player has completed it properly.

question: Your ball is on a steep slope in the general area. You carefully place your club behind the ball to address it, and the ball moves. Under the current Rules of Golf (2023), are you deemed to have caused the ball to move?
response:
RULING: Yes, you are deemed to have caused the ball to move under the current Rules of Golf.

The rule governing when a player is deemed to have caused a ball to move states that if a player's actions are a contributing cause of the ball moving, even if the movement would have happened anyway, the player is considered to have caused the movement. Placing your club behind the ball to address it on a steep slope is a contributing cause, even if the ball may have moved on its own due to the slope. Therefore, you are penalized one stroke, and the ball must be replaced on its original spot.

This applies anywhere on the course, not just the putting green. The "ball moved by natural forces" exception that applies on the putting green does not extend to other areas of the course. If a ball at rest moves due to the player's actions, such as addressing it, the player is responsible regardless of the ball's final location.

question: Your ball lands in a flower bed adjacent to the clubhouse. The flower bed is not marked as GUR and there is no local rule about it. Your ball is playable. A committee member walking by tells you that flower beds are always treated as GUR. Is the committee member correct?
response:
RULING: No, the committee member is incorrect. The flower bed is not a condition of the course that is defined as ground under repair, and there is no local rule in effect treating it as such.

The Rules of Golf define "ground under repair" as "any part of the course that the committee has marked or otherwise defined as ground under repair." Since this flower bed has not been marked or defined as GUR by the committee, it is considered part of the general area, and the player must play the ball as it lies.

There is no automatic or implied "always GUR" status for flower beds. The committee would need to have adopted a specific local rule to that effect in order for it to be treated as ground under repair. Without such a local rule in place, the player must play the ball from the flower bed under the normal rules for the general area.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a penalty area. Can you declare it unplayable and take unplayable ball relief (back-on-the-line or two club-lengths) under Rule 19?
response:
RULING: Yes, you can declare an unplayable ball in a penalty area and take unplayable ball relief under Rule 19.

The unplayable ball rule (Rule 19) applies anywhere on the course, including in a penalty area. If your ball is unplayable in the penalty area, you have three relief options under Rule 19:

1) Play the ball from where your previous stroke was made, under stroke and distance (Rule 19.2a)
2) Drop the ball within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball lay, not nearer the hole (Rule 19.2b)  
3) Drop the ball on a line from the hole through the spot where the ball lay, with no limit on how far back on that line you go (Rule 19.2c)

You do not have to prove the ball is unplayable in the penalty area - you can simply choose to declare it unplayable and take one of those three relief options, incurring a one-stroke penalty.

This gives you an additional way to get relief from an unplayable situation in a penalty area, beyond just the standard penalty area relief options (Rules 17.1d and 17.2).

question: Preferred lies (lift, clean, and place) are in effect via local rule. Your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway. Can you lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot take preferred lies relief for your ball in the first cut of rough just off the fairway.

The preferred lies local rule (Model Local Rule E-3) only applies to balls lying on the fairway or any area cut to fairway height or less. It does not apply to the rough, first cut, semi-rough, or any area not cut to fairway height. Your ball in the first cut of rough does not qualify for the preferred lies relief, even though the local rule is in effect.

The scope of the preferred lies local rule is limited to the fairway and areas cut to fairway height. A ball in the first cut of rough, even if very close to the fairway, is outside the area covered by this local rule. You must play the ball as it lies in the rough.
